3A_Bibliographic.citation: Doran, N., Wade, N. E., Courtney, K. E., Sullivan, R. M., & Jacobus, J. (2025). Mobile phone ownership, social media use, and substance use at ages 11-13 in the ABCD study. Addictive Behaviors, 161, 108211.

3B_Sample.N: 10,907 (all participants with substance use data at 1+ of 4 assessment points); sensitivity subsample n=9,628 (no prior substance use)
3B_Sample.timepoints_used: Year 2 annual (age 11-12, predictor baseline), Year 2 mid-year, Year 3 annual, Year 3 mid-year (ages ~12-14)

3C_Exposure.exposure_measure: Two binary self-report items: (1) Do you have your own mobile phone? (2) Do you have at least one social media account?
modalities_included: social media use (binary: any social media account)
3C_Exposure.how_operationalized: Binary (yes/no) at Year 2 annual assessment. 71.2% owned phones; 54.9% used social media. 66.7% of phone owners used social media.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Substance use: alcohol, nicotine/tobacco, cannabis (time-varying binary at each of 4 timepoints)
3D_Outcome.measure_used: Timeline Followback (annual assessments) and brief substance use questionnaire (mid-year assessments); combined into binary any-use-since-last-assessment variables

3E_Design_Analysis.design: Prospective longitudinal (Year 2 through Year 3 mid-year, ~18 months)
3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Generalized estimating equations with binomial distribution, logit link, autoregressive covariance. Separate models for each predictor x outcome combination (6 models). Tested predictor x time interactions; retained if significant. Sensitivity analyses in substance-naive subsample (n=9,628).
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex assigned at birth, racial/ethnic background (categorical: Black, Hispanic, Asian, Other, with non-Hispanic White reference), household income (ordinal 1-10)
3E_Design_Analysis.SES_in_model: yes - household income was a covariate in all models
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: To estimate associations between predictors and substance use outcomes over time, we utilized a longitudinal logistic regression model for correlated dichotomous outcomes, estimated using the generalized estimating equations (GEE) method

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Phone ownership -> alcohol use (main effect across study period)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: There was a main effect of phone ownership (b = 0.29 [95 % confidence interval 0.16, 0.41], Odds Ratio [OR] = 1.34 [95 % confidence interval 1.18, 1.51], p < 0.001), such that participants who owned phones at age 11-12 were about 34 % more likely to report any alcohol use across the study period.

There was a main effect of phone ownership (b = 0.53 [0.19, 0.87], OR = 1.71 (1.21, 2.40]), indicating that youth who owned mobile phones at age 11-12 were 71 % more likely to report nicotine/tobacco use throughout the study period.
cannabis use...did not differ depending for youth who did and did not own mobile phones at age 11-12 (b = 0.27 [-0.20, 0.74], OR = 1.31 [0.82, 2.09]).

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Social media -> alcohol use (main effect ns, but interaction with time significant)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: a non-significant main effect of social media (b = 0.04 [-0.18, 0.26], OR = 1.06 [0.92, 1.21])

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Social media x time interaction -> alcohol use (growing divergence over time)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: a significant interaction between the two (b = 0.09 [0.01, 0.16], OR = 1.09 [1.01, 1.017])
a significant main effect of social media use (b = 0.21 [0.06. 0.37], OR = 1.22 [1.06, 1.37])

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Social media x time interaction -> nicotine/tobacco (growing association over time)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: a significant interaction between the two (b = 0.08 [0.05, 0.11], OR = 1.08 [1.05, 1.11])

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Social media -> cannabis use (main effect; very large OR but very low base rate)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: the model of the association between age 11-12 social media use and cannabis use over time yielded a main effect of social media use status (b = 1.59 [1.04, 2.14], OR = 4.91 [2.84, 8.47])
3F_Effect_Sizes.below_threshold: no (OR 4.91 far exceeds 1.20; however cannabis prevalence <1% at baseline)

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: participants who at age 11-12 owned mobile phones but had never used any of the 3 substances were 28 % more likely to report alcohol use
92 % more likely to report nicotine/tobacco use over the next 18 months
participants who reported social media use and no prior substance use at age 11-12 were 61 % more likely to report alcohol use
312 % more likely to report nicotine/tobacco use
246 % more likely to report cannabis use over the next 18 months

3G_Interpretation.verbatim_recommendation: These findings highlight the importance of additional research into digital and non-digital interventions to reduce these risks. The ubiquity of mobile phone ownership and social media use suggest that interventions with a broad reach (e.g., school-based) are likely to be most impactful.

3H_Rhetorical.pattern_detail: significance-primary combined with modifiable-factor. Large ORs for cannabis (4.91) are emphasized but base rate is <1%, meaning absolute risk differences are tiny. The framing of '312% more likely' and '246% more likely' in the sensitivity analyses is technically accurate but rhetoically misleading given the extremely low base rates (nicotine ~0.9%, cannabis ~0.5%). Binary exposure measure (any social media yes/no) conflates all levels of use.
3H_Rhetorical.list_1_qualifier: yes - authors note 'the design of the present study does not permit us to conclude that mobile phone ownership or social media use at age 11-12 had a causal effect'

3I_Flags.ambiguity_notes: Binary exposure measures (own phone yes/no, have social media yes/no) are very crude. Cannabis OR of 4.91 is driven by extremely low base rates (<0.5% at baseline). No control for peer substance use or parental substance use. Different substance use measures at annual vs. mid-year assessments. Data collection overlapped with COVID-19 pandemic onset. Authors note confounding by unmeasured variables (peer/parent use, mental health).
